fault_hypothesis: The expiry check on line 83 uses strict greater-than (`now > effective`) instead of greater-than-or-equal (`now >= effective`), so when `now == effective` the key is incorrectly returned as valid instead of expired.

constraints.intent: When now equals effective, the condition `now > effective` is False so the key is not expired — this constraint is FALSE on the boundary-equality failing tests.
At the point effective is used in the comparison, now should never equal effective for a correctly-expiring key; equality means the bug will manifest.
After the else branch (key not expired), now must be strictly less than effective; if now == effective we should have returned None but did not.
After computing effective, if it equals now the subsequent strict comparison will fail to expire the key, revealing the off-by-one boundary bug.
